lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market contractors providing repeat or recurring outsourced building-finishing work to external customers, especially commercial waterproofing, concrete coating or sealing, modular furniture and exhibit installation, and recurring shade, blind, fixture, and space-reconfiguration installation.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off-only new-construction or residential installation businesses without a repeat customer base, captive internal installation units, manufacturers whose primary economics are product sales, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project and work-order revenue from property owners, facilities managers, general contractors, dealers, exhibitors, and commercial tenants; eligible firms earn repeat demand through maintenance, renovation cycles, multi-site programs, reconfigurations, or recurring events, commonly under competitively bid fixed-price or unit-price scopes.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines waterproofing and coating maintenance, modular-office and trade-show installation, and several one-time residential or construction installation activities. To keep one coherent screen, the lens retains only firms with demonstrable repeat commercial, maintenance, multi-site, or recurring-event demand and excludes one-off-only installers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for the broader 238300 Building Finishing Contractors parent, where the largest occupations are predominantly field trades and labor but also include supervisors, general and operations managers, and office clerks. Microsoft measures real-world generative-AI applicability by occupation and finds the strongest applicability in information, writing, office, and communication work. I map those administrative, estimating, scheduling, documentation, customer-communication, and supervisory task slices into a wage-weighted share for 238390. The parent-industry mix can misstate this miscellaneous code in either direction; chatbot evidence may understate future integrated agents but overstate true labor substitution because assistance is not headcount avoidance.
a.rationale: Physical installation remains the majority of work, but the relatively well-paid coordination layer and clerical work create a meaningful exposed share. The base allows substantial exposure within office and management roles plus a small documentation and planning component within field supervision, while leaving hands-on fabrication, preparation, access, application, installation, inspection, and cleanup largely unexposed.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS occupation-by-wage file for 238390, combined with employer time studies that split estimator, project-manager, dispatcher, clerical, and field-supervisor hours into automatable tasks, would materially change the range.
caveats: OEWS publishes the useful occupation mix at parent 238300 rather than the exact six-digit code.
The Microsoft applicability measure is based on Copilot conversations and is not a measure of substitution or avoided hiring.
The frozen compensation ratio is LOW quality, measured at ancestor 23839, uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, and is harmonized to the IPS basis; this primitive does not revise it.

rho.bridge: The 2026 AGC-Sage construction survey reports AI deployment across construction firms, including 45% for office and administrative functions, 23% for estimating, and 20% for design or preconstruction. I use those present adoption rates as a floor and extend them to a five-year implementation share of the exposed opportunity. The survey covers construction broadly and likely includes firms larger and more technologically prepared than the target; that biases readiness upward, while using current deployment to represent a five-year horizon biases it downward.
rho.rationale: Many exposed workflows use digital documents and standard business systems, so implementation can proceed without automating the physical jobsite. Five-year realization remains limited by fragmented data, inconsistent job coding, small-firm change capacity, human review of bids and scopes, field-to-office handoffs, and the cost of workflow integration.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of $1-10M-EBITDA specialty contractors linking specific AI workflows to adoption, labor hours, error rates, and sustained headcount avoidance after 12 months would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Survey deployment percentages do not measure workflow depth, realized labor savings, or persistence.
General-contractor respondents may have more formal preconstruction and IT functions than small finishing contractors.

e.rationale: The official definition spans both repeat-friendly activities such as waterproofing, coating, modular-furniture reconfiguration, and trade-show installation and one-time installation scopes such as closets, countertops, and residential fixtures. The base assumes a modest majority of LMM firms have enough repeat commercial, maintenance, dealer, multi-site, or event demand to fit the narrowed lens, but no source allocates firms or receipts across these activities and revenue models.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 238390 firms in D&B/Hoovers or Data Axle, matched to websites, customer concentration, maintenance contracts, repeat-revenue share, and primary activity, would directly estimate eligible-firm share.
caveats: NAICS classifies establishments by primary activity, not by recurrence, customer concentration, or revenue model.
The frozen LMM firm count of 116 is an ESTIMATE margin-bridged from size-class counts and average receipts; this primitive does not revise it.

s5.bridge: BizBuySell reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings in its benchmark set and a 207-day median time on market, demonstrating an active transfer channel but not supplying the population denominator or a six-digit cut. Census ABS provides owner-age data for employer firms, but the public landing page does not convert that age distribution into control-transfer incidence. I combine this selected construction transaction evidence with bounded judgment about succession and sale over five years. Voluntary broker reporting and marketplace selection likely undercount private off-market transfers but overrepresent firms already prepared and willing to sell.
s5.rationale: Transferability exists because construction businesses do sell, yet five-year control transfer is not the same as owner retirement intent, a listing, closure, death, or internal reorganization. Owner dependence, customer relationships, bonding and licensing, uneven records, and concentration around key estimators or supervisors reduce the fraction that closes as a transferable operation.
s5.change_evidence: Longitudinal private-company ownership records for 238390 firms, matched to closed transaction databases such as DealStats or GF Data and to owner-age/succession interviews, would directly estimate qualifying five-year control transfers.
caveats: BizBuySell data are voluntarily reported and mainly represent smaller marketed businesses, not the full LMM population.
The sold-listing total spans building and construction broadly and does not provide an annual hazard rate.
ABS owner age is evidence of an observable succession indicator, not evidence that an owner will sell.

q.rationale: AIA Contract Documents describes stipulated-sum or fixed-price contracting as the most common owner-contractor form. That structure lets a contractor retain savings on already-priced work, but frequent project turnover, rebidding, renewal negotiation, transparent labor assumptions, and competition should pass part of a durable productivity gain to customers. The range discounts retention over five years and does not count demand growth or implementation difficulty.
q.change_evidence: Matched bid histories and job-cost ledgers from a panel of finishing contractors, including renewal pricing, gross-margin bridges, win rates, and customer sharing before and after workflow automation, would materially change retention.
caveats: No source measures AI-benefit pass-through for 238390.
Contract mix varies across residential, commercial subcontract, service-call, dealer, and event work; cost-plus scopes retain less benefit than fixed-price scopes.

d5.bridge: AIA's July 2026 consensus forecast covers nominal nonresidential building spending, showing a 0.3% decline in 2026 and 3.0% growth in 2027 with wide sector dispersion, while BLS projects construction laborers and helpers to grow 7% from 2024 to 2034 and ties demand to overall construction, renovation, and repair activity. I translate those broader nominal spending and occupational signals into a five-year constant-price quantity ratio for the mixed 238390 service basket. Nominal spending overstates real quantity when input prices rise, but labor projections may understate output if productivity improves.
d5.rationale: The base is essentially flat real demand: weak near-term building activity is offset by renovation, repair, waterproofing, reconfiguration, and eventual construction recovery. The wide range reflects exposure to residential and office softness on one side and data centers, health care, recreation, maintenance, and building reuse on the other.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year real construction-put-in-place forecasts split by renovation versus new build and by end market, plus 238390 Economic Census receipts by type of construction and maintenance/repair share, would materially narrow the range.
caveats: AIA covers nonresidential buildings and is nominal, while 238390 also serves residential and recurring event markets.
BLS occupational growth covers construction laborers and helpers across industries, not output of this industry.
The code's service mix is too broad for a single end-market forecast to represent every firm.

o.bridge: Census defines the industry around on-site finishing work, and BLS reports that 91% of construction and extraction workers are exposed to outdoor work and describes laborer demand as tied to construction and renovation while noting that prefabricated components can reduce some helper demand. I translate the physical, site-specific work requirement into the share of year-five quantity still needing an accountable contractor. The broader occupational evidence may overstate on-site intensity for modular furniture and exhibits, while the activity definition may understate future off-site fabrication and self-installation.
o.rationale: Waterproofing, coating, sealing, fitting, attachment, installation, dismantling, access, substrate preparation, safety, quality control, and warranty responsibility remain site-specific. Software can simplify design, quoting, procurement, and coordination, and prefabrication or customer self-installation can remove some quantity, but most completed work still requires a local accountable operator.
o.change_evidence: Activity-level forecasts of factory-prefabricated and self-installable product penetration, robotic coating or sealing deployments, contractor-of-record requirements, and customer make-versus-buy behavior in each 238390 niche would materially move the range.
caveats: The 91% outdoor-work statistic is for the broad construction and extraction occupational group, and some included finishing work is indoors.
Operator requirement differs sharply between waterproofing, recurring exhibits, modular furniture, and simple fixture installation.

narrative.executive_view: Other building finishing is a mixed collection of locally delivered physical services. The clearest AI opportunity is in the coordination layer around field work, while the clearest strategic constraint is that only part of the code has repeat commercial or maintenance demand and transferable operations independent of the owner.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft scopes and proposals, extract requirements, assist takeoffs and estimates, schedule crews, answer routine customer questions, organize field documentation, flag job-cost variance, and accelerate invoicing. It is much less able to replace substrate assessment, site access, physical preparation and installation, safety decisions, inspection, rework, or accountable sign-off.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed-price work can preserve early productivity gains inside the contractor, especially between bid and completion. Over repeated bids and renewals, customers and competitors are likely to capture part of the benefit through sharper pricing, faster turnaround, or higher service expectations, so retention should be meaningful but incomplete.
narrative.firm_availability: There is an active market for construction-business transfers, but the qualifying event rate is uncertain and published marketplace data lack a population denominator. The best targets are firms with repeat accounts, delegated estimating and supervision, credible job-cost records, transferable licenses or qualifying personnel, and limited dependence on the seller's personal relationships.
narrative.demand_durability: Real demand is likely to be broadly stable over five years but uneven by niche and end market. Renovation, repair, waterproofing, facility reconfiguration, health care, recreation, and selected growth construction can offset weakness in traditional offices or rate-sensitive residential work; most delivered quantity should still require an on-site operator.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The six-digit code hides materially different business models, and no public source provides its exact occupation mix, recurring-revenue share, AI pass-through, or transfer hazard. Cyclicality, bid competition, owner dependence, customer concentration, bonding or licensing, rework, warranty claims, and weak field data can erase benefits. A portfolio tilted toward one-off new construction would be materially less coherent than the narrowed lens.
narrative.principal_driver: A repeat-service contractor can apply AI to a meaningful administrative and coordination layer while preserving the site-specific operator role.
narrative.principal_weakness: Industry heterogeneity and the absence of six-digit evidence on repeat revenue and transferable control events make eligible-firm availability highly uncertain.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 238390 Other Building Finishing Contractors
sources.supports: Defines the industry's included and excluded finishing activities and states that work may include new work, additions, alterations, maintenance, and repairs.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Lists the largest occupations in parent 238300 Building Finishing Contractors, led by field trades and labor and also including supervisors, general and operations managers, and office clerks.

sources.title: Occupational Requirements in the United States, 2024
sources.supports: Reports physical and environmental job requirements, including outdoor exposure for 91.0% of construction and extraction workers.

sources.title: 2026 Construction Industry Outlook
sources.supports: Reports AGC-Sage construction survey findings that 45% of firms deploy AI in office and administrative functions, 23% in estimating, and 20% in design or preconstruction.

sources.title: Working with AI: Measuring the Applicability of Generative AI to Occupations
sources.supports: Analyzes 200,000 Copilot conversations and finds the strongest AI applicability in knowledge, office and administrative, sales, information-gathering, writing, teaching, and advising activities.

sources.title: Four Types of Construction Contracts You Need to Understand
sources.supports: States that stipulated-sum, lump-sum, or fixed-price contracting is the most common agreement form between an owner and contractor.

sources.title: Construction Business Valuation Benchmarks
sources.supports: Reports 3,142 sold building-and-construction listings in its benchmark data and a 207-day median time on market, documenting an active but selected transfer channel.

sources.title: 2024 Annual Business Survey: Characteristics of Business Owners
sources.supports: Confirms that the 2024 ABS covers reference year 2023 and provides an Age of Owner table for employer businesses.

sources.title: July 2026 Consensus Construction Forecast
sources.supports: Forecasts nominal nonresidential building spending down 0.3% in 2026 and up 3.0% in 2027, with substantial divergence across end markets.

sources.title: Construction Laborers and Helpers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Projects 7% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, links demand to construction and renovation, and notes that prefabricated components can reduce demand for some helpers.
